overall_summary: Talen opposes FERC jurisdiction over large load interconnections to preserve federal-state balance under the FPA and rejects the IMM's BYOG proposal, arguing it violates open-access principles and discriminates against existing generation. Instead, Talen urges market-based solutions centered on improving the Reliability Backstop Auction to send clear price signals and attract needed generation capacity.

quotes.text: Talen Energy Corporation ("Talen") files these reply comments to encourage the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission ("Commission" or "FERC") to proceed with caution in any action taken following the proposed Advance Notice of Proposed Rulemaking ("ANOPR") in order to preserve the federal/state jurisdictional balance established under the Federal Power Act ("FPA") and decades of precedent.
quotes.concern: FERC should proceed cautiously to preserve federal-state jurisdictional balance

quotes.text: FERC should also ensure that any action it takes does not result in undue discrimination against existing generation resources that are critical for reliability.
quotes.concern: Oppose policies that discriminate against existing generation

quotes.text: the Commission should soundly reject proposals—such as those advanced by the Independent Market Monitor of PJM Interconnection, L.L.C. ("IMM")—that would grant PJM Interconnection, L.L.C. ("PJM") "discretion" to discriminate in favor of "bring your own generation" ("BYOG") arrangements or selectively restrict load interconnection.
quotes.concern: Reject IMM proposal for PJM discretion to favor BYOG or restrict load

quotes.text: These ill-advised measures conflict with open-access principles, risk fragmenting competitive markets, and undermine the reliability of the transmission system as a whole.
quotes.concern: BYOG proposals violate open access and fragment markets

quotes.text: the act of interconnecting a large load with the transmission system encompasses more than transmission service alone. For end-use customers receiving retail service, the delivery of energy—particularly for large loads—is inseparable from retail distribution, which falls squarely within state jurisdiction.
quotes.concern: Large load interconnection involves retail distribution falling under state jurisdiction

quotes.text: The Commission's jurisdiction is expressly limited to "the transmission of electric energy in interstate commerce and the sale of electric energy at wholesale in interstate commerce."
quotes.concern: FERC authority is limited to wholesale transmission, not retail

quotes.text: Such disputes would undermine the cooperative federalism framework of the FPA, delay regulatory certainty under Sections 205 and 206, and hinder timely development of new generation resources needed to meet reliability challenges.
quotes.concern: Jurisdictional disputes harm federalism framework and delay regulatory certainty

quotes.text: The Commission should ensure no existing generation resources face undue discrimination in any action taken.
quotes.concern: Prevent undue discrimination against existing generation

quotes.text: Proposals that require new large load customers to bring their own new generation are discriminatory.
quotes.concern: BYOG requirements are discriminatory

quotes.text: enhanced reliability studies that apply disproportionate requirements to large loads contracting with existing generation as opposed to new generation are unlawful and will result in undue discrimination against existing generation.
quotes.concern: Disproportionate reliability requirements for existing generation are unlawful

quotes.text: Instead, the Commission should allow existing generation resources to compete on equal footing with new generation resources to serve large load customers.
quotes.concern: Existing and new generation should compete on equal terms

quotes.text: policies favoring new generation at the expense of existing resources risks accelerating retirement of highly reliable, dispatchable, and baseload units.
quotes.concern: Policies favoring new generation cause premature retirement of baseload units

quotes.text: maintaining resource adequacy is not simply about adding new capacity; it requires a balanced approach that preserves existing assets while integrating new resources to meet fast-growing demand.
quotes.concern: Resource adequacy requires balance between existing and new generation

quotes.text: This will not happen if the Commission creates policies that favor only new resources that distort market signals and undermine reliability.
quotes.concern: Policies favoring only new resources distort market signals

quotes.text: To ensure all load is served, new and existing resources must be allowed to compete on a level playing field, without structural preferences.
quotes.concern: No structural preferences between new and existing resources

quotes.text: Conditioning customer interconnections on BYOG or instructing PJM to deny service conflicts with bedrock open-access principles FERC established in Order No. 888 and Order No. 2000, which mandate non-discriminatory transmission service under a Commission-approved tariff.
quotes.concern: BYOG conditioning violates open-access principles and Orders 888 and 2000

quotes.text: Such policies would create structural preferences, undermine competitive markets, and introduce regulatory uncertainty, which would in turn deter investment and fragment resource planning.
quotes.concern: BYOG policies create structural preferences and regulatory uncertainty

quotes.text: Reliability and market efficiency are best achieved through coordinated regional planning and market-based mechanisms that provide clear price signals—not through ad hoc and discriminatory interconnection restrictions.
quotes.concern: Market-based mechanisms with clear price signals achieve reliability

quotes.text: Instructing PJM to pick winners and losers among loads would undermine the Commission's open-access framework and invite litigation over undue discrimination, delaying urgently needed reliability solutions.
quotes.concern: PJM picking winners among loads violates open access

quotes.text: Selectively restricting interconnection rights or conditioning them on self-supply undermines these principles and introduces structural preferences that will distort market outcomes.
quotes.concern: Selective restrictions create structural preferences

quotes.text: Such an approach would effectively create a two-tiered load system—one for loads that can finance generation and another for those that cannot—which is contrary to the Commission's long-standing policy of ensuring equal access to transmission service.
quotes.concern: BYOG creates two-tiered access to transmission service

quotes.text: From a reliability perspective, prioritizing BYOG arrangements does not guarantee system stability and could harm resource adequacy.
quotes.concern: BYOG prioritization does not ensure reliability

quotes.text: Prioritizing BYOG arrangements will also create two tiers of generation— new and existing.
quotes.concern: BYOG prioritization creates tiers between new and existing generation

quotes.text: Conditioning interconnection on BYOG would also fragment planning processes, discourage participation in centralized capacity markets, and potentially lead to inefficient siting of generation resources.
quotes.concern: BYOG fragments planning and discourages centralized markets

quotes.text: The way to ensure all loads are reliably served is by ensuring there are sufficient generation resources to meet the forecasted exponential growth in demand.
quotes.concern: Focus on sufficient generation resources for growing demand

quotes.text: The fundamental underlying issue of resource adequacy will not be solved solely through a rulemaking process addressing large load interconnection.
quotes.concern: Large load rulemaking cannot solve resource adequacy alone

quotes.text: Supporting market-based mechanisms designed to ensure sufficient generation resources are available to serve load demand is more pressing (and within the Commission's jurisdiction) than the issues raised by the ANOPR.
quotes.concern: Market-based mechanisms for resource adequacy are priority

quotes.text: To facilitate continued resource adequacy, including both building new generation in a timely manner and retaining existing resources, the Commission must ensure that price signals accurately reflect scarcity and reliability needs.
quotes.concern: Price signals must reflect scarcity and reliability needs

quotes.text: Only when markets send clear signals can developers make informed investment backed decisions.
quotes.concern: Clear market signals enable informed investment decisions

quotes.text: PJM's Open Access Transmission Tariff ("OATT") already contains an efficient, market-based solution—the Reliability Backstop Auction ("RBA")—that, with modifications, can address issues of resource adequacy by sending accurate price signals to generation developers.
quotes.concern: RBA mechanism with modifications can address resource adequacy

quotes.text: the RBA process is a market-based mechanism designed to guarantee sufficient generation will be available to ensure system reliability when there is a lack of sufficient capacity committed through the Reliability Pricing Model ("RPM") auctions.
quotes.concern: RBA guarantees sufficient generation when RPM is insufficient

quotes.text: Talen suggests modifying the RBA so that the auction is triggered when the RPM auctions clear below 98% of the reserve margin requirement two base residual auctions in a row, rather than the current trigger of the base residual auctions clearing below 99% of the reserve margin three auctions in a row.
quotes.concern: Lower RBA trigger threshold to two auctions at 98% reserve margin

quotes.text: The RBA's guarantee of a multi-year term will stimulate generation development, enabling developers to respond quickly to meet the communicated resource adequacy needs.
quotes.concern: Multi-year RBA terms attract generation development

quotes.text: the Commission should direct PJM to develop a Reliability Resource Initiative-like interconnection process which will expedite the interconnection of all resources selected under the RBA mechanism.
quotes.concern: Expedite interconnection of RBA-selected resources

quotes.text: Expediting interconnection of resources selected under the RBA mechanism will guarantee that projects developed expressly to meet resource adequacy needs are able to bring the additional energy resources onto the network and relieve reliability concerns as quickly as possible.
quotes.concern: Fast-track RBA projects onto network to address reliability

quotes.text: the Commission should order all RTOs/ISOs to develop similar market-based mechanisms.
quotes.concern: All RTOs/ISOs should adopt similar market-based mechanisms

bins.name: Preserve federal-state jurisdictional balance
bins.description: Large load interconnection involves retail distribution and customer service that fall under exclusive state jurisdiction per FPA Section 201(b). FERC jurisdiction is limited to wholesale transmission. Asserting jurisdiction would trigger litigation, undermine cooperative federalism, and delay development of new generation resources.

bins.name: Expedited interconnection for resource adequacy
bins.description: Expedite interconnection processes for resources selected under market-based mechanisms like the RBA to quickly bring capacity online and address resource adequacy needs.

bins.name: Resource adequacy transcends load interconnection rulemaking
bins.description: The fundamental resource adequacy issue cannot be solved through a large load interconnection rulemaking alone. Market-based mechanisms for ensuring sufficient generation are the priority.

bins.name: Market-based mechanisms with clear price signals
bins.description: Support market-based mechanisms like the improved RBA that send clear price signals reflecting scarcity and reliability needs. These signals enable informed investment decisions and stimulate timely generation development. Multi-year RBA commitments attract generation capacity to meet resource adequacy.

bins.name: Modify RBA trigger and expedite interconnection
bins.description: Lower the RBA trigger threshold to two base residual auctions at 98 percent reserve margin (versus current three auctions at 99 percent) to better respond to growing demand. Expedite interconnection of RBA-selected resources using a Reliability Resource Initiative-like process. Extend market-based mechanisms across all RTOs/ISOs.

bins.name: Regional planning and generation resource adequacy
bins.description: Support coordinated regional planning focused on ensuring sufficient generation resources for fast-growing demand. Balance must preserve existing assets while integrating new resources. Prioritize analysis of how market mechanisms attract and retain needed generation capacity.

bins.name: Open-access principles non-negotiable
bins.description: BYOG and selective interconnection restrictions violate bedrock open-access principles established in Orders 888 and 2000 mandating non-discriminatory transmission service. Such policies effectively create two-tiered access to transmission and undermine the Commission's long-standing commitment to equal access.

bins.name: Preserve existing generation resources
bins.description: Policies favoring only new generation risk accelerating premature retirement of highly reliable, dispatchable, and baseload units. Maintaining resource adequacy requires preserving existing generation while integrating new capacity to meet fast-growing demand.

bins.name: Price signals must reflect true scarcity and reliability
bins.description: Market signals must accurately reflect scarcity and reliability needs to attract generation investment. Fragmented planning and selective interconnection restrictions distort market outcomes and prevent efficient siting of generation resources.

bins.name: BYOG does not guarantee reliability
bins.description: Prioritizing BYOG arrangements does not guarantee system stability and could harm resource adequacy by creating tiers between new and existing generation, reducing investment signals for needed capacity.

bins.name: Strengthen Reliability Backstop Auction mechanism
bins.description: The RBA already provides an efficient market-based solution to guarantee sufficient generation when RPM auctions are inadequate. Lower the trigger threshold and accelerate interconnection of RBA-selected resources. Extend similar mechanisms across all RTOs/ISOs to ensure timely development of needed capacity.

bins.name: Reject BYOG and prevent discrimination against existing generation
bins.description: Reject BYOG requirements or PJM discretion to selectively restrict load interconnection as they discriminate against existing generation and violate open-access principles. Such policies create structural preferences, fragment planning, and distort market signals. Existing and new generation should compete on equal footing to serve large loads and ensure resource adequacy.
